message.content:
You are a CLEAN-ROOM implementer under the campaign protocol at /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md — read it first and comply strictly.
ACCESS RULES: You must NOT read any file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/crates/, and NOT read any Haskell source (*.hs) of tamarin-prover anywhere on disk (notably /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover/{lib,src}/ and /home/kamilner/tamarin-prover-testing/{lib,src}/). No web access of any kind. You MAY: run the reference binary/servers via your cluster's oracle scripts (export PATH="$PATH:/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin" if invoking directly — maude/dot live there), read .spthy example files, and read/write freely inside your own cluster directory under /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/. Do not modify anything outside your cluster directory. Do not run git commits.
DISCIPLINE: log every oracle interaction by appending to your cluster's workspace QUERIES.log; record every derived behavioral fact in BEHAVIOR.md with provenance; append a round section to a REPORT file in the workspace. Code comments must describe current behavior only. Every behavioral claim must trace to a logged probe or a captured fixture — never to model-memory of tamarin internals. Return a PLAIN-TEXT summary (no structured output).

UNIT B (graph generation), round 6 — close the record-cell wrap TRIGGER, the one gap blocking adoption. Live-server ports: 3200-3299 only.
Cluster: /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/ — read workspace BEHAVIOR.md (your §3f documents this residual honestly), QUERIES.log Session 5, REPORTs. Crate: workspace/graph-clean.

Your wrap_cell triggers on a cell's OWN flat width > 87. That is wrong in general: the reference decides per-cell within the WHOLE record line — a cell of flat width 25 (e.g. Ack( ~n.4, <x1.4, x2.4> )) wraps when it sits deep on a wide record line, and corpus first-line widths span 19..277, so neither 87 nor any per-cell constant is the trigger. Characterize the true record-level rule with controlled live probes: fix a target cell and systematically vary the number and widths of PRECEDING cells on the same record line (and the rule name/header width), bisect the exact accumulated column at which the target flips from flat to wrapped; test whether the decision for one cell depends on FOLLOWING cells (lookahead) or only on what precedes; check interaction with the per-cell fill width you already pinned (a wrapped cell's continuation lines pack greedily — re-verify at record level). Mine the 12022-payload corpus (144k+ wide records) to validate the candidate rule at scale, then implement record-level layout in build_record (and the RawRule pre-rendered path) so wrap decisions are byte-exact end to end.
OPPORTUNISTIC (only if the trigger is closed with time left): cluster colors are currently caller inputs — mine corpus role-name→color pairs and probe novel role names live to test whether the color is derivable from the name alone; implement if derivable, else document the negative.
The full GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS round-trip must stay 12022/12022. Stop all servers when done.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll start by reading the protocol and my cluster's existing state before touching anything.

content.content:
1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).
47

content.text: The workspace files are what I need. Let me read the workspace BEHAVIOR.md, REPORT2.md, QUERIES.log, and SPEC files.

content.content: 1 # BEHAVIOR.md — observed spec of the tamarin web-UI constraint-system graph payload 2 3 All statements below are derived from OBSERVED oracle output: the 81 captured 4 crawl manifests under `oracle/captured_responses/` (14705 graph payloads) and 5 targeted live probing of the black-box HS binary (see QUERIES.log). No 6 tamarin-prover source was read. Where a behavior could not be pinned down 7 black-box it is marked **[GAP]**. 8 9 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 10 ## 1. Transport / payload family 11 12 A crawl manifest is JSON: `{base, lemmas, log, capped, manifest}` where 13 `manifest` maps a URL to `{kind, status, body}` (kind ∈ html|text|json|dot). 14 15 Graph-bearing URL families (theory index `N` replaces `#` in captures): 16 17 | URL family | kind | payload | 18 |---|---|---| 19 | `/thy/trace/N/interactive-graph-def/proof/[/...]` | `dot` | **the graph** — graphviz DOT text (`text/plain`) | 20 | `/thy/trace/N/intdot/proof/[/...]` | `html` | wrapper page embedding `` | 21 | `/thy/trace/N/main/cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` | `json` | `{html,title}`; html contains `` | 22 23 The DOT text at `interactive-graph-def` is the whole deliverable. `intdot` is a 24 fixed HTML shell (only the `` and the `dotsrc` path vary). The graph is 25 rendered client-side from the DOT by `/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js`. 26 27 **JSON graph payload** [GAP]: the binary exposes a *separate* `--with-json` 28 rendering backend (`tamarin-prover interactive --help`). No JSON rendering tool 29 is installed here and no `interactive-graph-def` body in the corpus is JSON — 30 the entire corpus is the DOT backend. JSON graph format is therefore unobserved. 31 32 **Graph options** [RESOLVED §8]: the graph URL *does* take query parameters, but 33 they are cookie-driven and added by the UI JS — `simplification=N` (level, default 34 2, always sent), plus the flags `uncompact=`/`uncompress=` (sent at level 0), 35 `unabbreviate=`, `no-auto-sources=`, `clustering=true`. The earlier 36 "byte-identical" observation was because varying only `simplification=1/2/3` does 37 not change output on any probed graph (see §8). The real transforms are triggered 38 by the boolean flags. See §8. 39 40 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 41 ## 2. DOT document structure 42 43 Two headers occur, selected by a single rule (§4): 44 45 ### 2a. Simple header (non-clustered) 46 ``` 47 digraph "G" { 48 nodesep="0.3"; 49 ranksep="0.3"; 50 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2"]; 51 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica"]; 52 <statements…> 53 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 ### 2b. Compact header (clustered) 58 ``` 59 digraph "G" { 60 nodesep="0.8"; 61 ranksep="0.8"; 62 sep="4"; 63 splines="true"; 64 overlap="false"; 65 pack="true"; 66 packmode="cluster"; 67 concentrate="true"; 68 compound="true"; 69 remincross="true"; 70 mclimit="10"; 71 nslimit="20"; 72 nslimit1="20"; 73 ordering="out"; 74 rankdir="TB"; 75 showboxes="false"; 76 clusterrank="local"; 77 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2",margin="0.05,0.05",shape="ellipse"]; 78 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",penwidth="1.5",arrowsize="0.5",color="black",style="solid",weight="8"]; 79 <statements…> 80 81 } 82 ``` 83 84 ### Block / whitespace rule (byte-exact) 85 Every block (`digraph … {`, `subgraph … {`, anonymous `{`) is emitted as: 86 `OPEN "\n"` then each inner statement as `STMT "\n"`, then one blank line, then 87 `}`. i.e. the body always ends `…\n\n}`. The top-level digraph is followed by a 88 final `\n`. Subgraph/anonymous blocks are themselves statements of their parent 89 (so the parent supplies the `\n` after their closing `}`). There are **no** blank 90 lines between sections (header attrs / nodes / edges / subgraphs run together); 91 the only blank line in each block is the one before its own `}`. 92 An empty graph is header attrs + blank + `}` (154 bytes). 93 94 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 95 ## 3. Node & edge syntax 96 97 Only three node shapes appear in proof graphs: `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 98 99 ### 3a. Record node (a rule / graph-node instance) 100 ``` 101 n<ID>[shape="record",label="{{<p> cell|<p> cell}|{<p> cell}|{<p> cell|<p> cell}}",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="black|white",role="<Role>"]; 102 ``` 103 - Attribute order is fixed: `shape,label,fillcolor,style,fontcolor,role`. 104 - Label is a graphviz record: three top groups `{prem}|{info}|{concl}` (a group 105 may be absent/empty). Each group = cells joined by `|`. Each cell = 106 `<portid> text` (port id like `n0`, a space, then the fact text). Ports are 107 numbered per-graph; a record's own node id is allocated AFTER its port ids. 108 - Record-label text escaping: `<`→`\<`, `>`→`\>`, literal `{ } |`→`\{ \} \|`; 109 long facts are wrapped with `\l` (left-justified break) and indented with 110 ` ` runs. (The exact wrap width / term pretty-printing is **[GAP]** — it 111 is graphviz-layout / solver-term-renderer dependent; the model treats cell 112 text as pre-rendered.) 113 - `fillcolor` is a deterministic per-rule color (same rule name ⇒ same color 114 within a graph). `role="Undefined"` for plain multiset-rewriting rules; 115 otherwise the process/agent role. `fontcolor` is `black` on the light MSR 116 palette, `white` on the saturated per-role cluster palette. Exact color hash 117 is **[GAP]**; the model carries colors explicitly. 118 119 ### 3b. Ellipse node (atomic / knowledge / action / temporal node) 120 ``` 121 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse"]; 122 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse",color="<c>"]; 123 ``` 124 - Attribute order: `label,shape[,color]`. Optional `color` (`gray` for `!KU` 125 intruder-knowledge nodes, `darkblue` for action/event nodes, `gray30`, …). 126 - Ellipse text is NOT record-escaped (`<`,`>` appear literally, e.g. 127 `Session( 'I', <pk(~ltkA), pkB>, … )`). 128 129 ### 3c. Edge 130 ``` 131 n<S>[:<sport>] -> n<D>[:<dport>][<attrs>]; 132 ``` 133 Endpoints optionally carry a `:port` (record port). `<attrs>` is a 134 comma-separated attribute list, in observed key order. Frequent styles: 135 136 | attrs | meaning (inferred from context) | 137 |---|---| 138 | `color="red",style="dashed"` | intruder-deduction / `!KU` edge | 139 | `style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"` | structural premise/conclusion edge | 140 | `style="bold",weight="10.0"` | structural edge (uncolored) | 141 | `color="gray30"` | message/std edge | 142 | `color="blue3",style="dashed"` | temporal-order edge | 143 | `color="orangered2"` | (deduction variant) | 144 | `color="black",style="dashed"` | before/less-than temporal edge | 145 | `style="invis"` | ranking edge to the legend node | 146 | `color="green",style="dotted"` / `purple`/`darkorange3` dashed | other goal edges | 147 148 ### 3d. Node shapes (§ Round-3 item 2) 149 Corpus census (all 12 022 dot + 81 manifests): only `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 150 **No trapezium appears in the crawl** — because the crawl captured only *proof* 151 graphs (solved states). Live probing (NAXOS_eCK, NSLPK3 case-distinction graphs) 152 adds one more shape: 153 - **`invtrapezium`** — a `(#var, idx)` placeholder for an **unresolved graph node** 154 referenced by a still-open premise, e.g. `n9[label="(#i, 0)",shape="invtrapezium"]`. 155 `#var` is the (temporal) node variable, `idx` the premise index; the node is fed 156 by a structural edge from the conclusion that must satisfy that premise 157 (`n5:n2 -> n9[style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"]`). Appears in both the 158 compressed default and the uncompressed variant, in every source-case graph 159 tested. Attr order = ellipse's (`label,shape`). 160 - **`trapezium`** (spec-named dual: an unresolved *source* node feeding a present 161 premise) was **NOT observed** in any probe (Session-3 NAXOS/NSLPK3 case graphs, 162 Session-5 re-scan of NSLPK3 `cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` for i≤12,j≤8) nor anywhere 163 in the corpus/manifests. The invtrapezium is the *conclusion→absent-node* case 164 (its edge points **into** it: `n5:n2 -> n6`), i.e. the "conclusion feeding a node 165 absent from the graph"; the un-seen dual would be the mirror (`absent -> premise`). 166 Recorded unobserved; `GraphNode::Shaped{label,shape,color}` (generic) can emit any 167 shape a caller supplies. 168 169 ### 3g. Bare-timepoint ellipse + the `#last` designated timepoint (§ Round-5 item 3) 170 An uncolored ellipse whose label is a bare timepoint variable `#<var>`: 171 `n<ID>[label="#<var>",shape="ellipse"];`. Corpus census of ellipse labels found, 172 besides the `!KU(..)@#t` / `#t:rule[..]` / `Action(..)@#t` forms, bare-timepoint 173 nodes `#i` (1328), `#decrypt` (790), `#t0…#t6`, `#j`, `#j.1`, and the **designated 174 last timepoint `#last`** (107, e.g. `24a119958f784d43.dot`). `#last` arises when a 175 constraint system carries a last timepoint (induction / trace-property proofs); it 176 is the target of a `color="black",style="dashed"` before-edge. All render as the 177 plain uncolored ellipse above. Modelled as `GraphNode::Temporal{var}` (with 178 `GraphNode::last()` for `#last`). 179 180 ### 3e. Node-id / port allocation (§ Round-3 item 3) — byte-verified over 12 022 181 One global monotonic counter, ids handed out in **emission order** so file order == 182 id order. For each node in order: 183 - a **record** takes one id per cell (in cell order: premises, then the info cell, 184 then conclusions) and **then** one id for the node itself — so a `k`-cell record 185 occupies `n<p>…n<p+k-1>` (ports) and `n<p+k>` (node); 186 - every other kind (ellipse / plain / invtrapezium) takes exactly one id. 187 188 Example: a 4-prem/1-info/3-concl record takes ports `n0…n6`, node `n7`; the next 189 ellipse is `n8`. Verified 12022/12022 in `mine_ids.py` and again in Rust 190 (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`, via `NodeIdAllocator`). 191 192 ### 3f. Record-cell term rendering + line wrapping (§ Round-3 item 1) 193 **Group structure** (byte-verified over 160 409 records): the label is 194 `{grp}|{grp}|{grp}` where the groups are the *non-empty* ones among 195 `[premises, info, conclusions]`, in that order; the **info** group is always 196 present (100 %). An empty premise or conclusion group is dropped (a source rule 197 renders `{info}|{concl}`). 198 - **Info cell**: `#<temporal> : <RuleName>` and, if the rule has action facts, 199 `[<action>, …]` (e.g. `#i : I_Complete[Complete( … ), …]`, or bare `#vf.5 : Fresh`). 200 - **Premise/conclusion cell**: a single fact. 201 - **Fact spacing**: a fact pads — `Name( a, b )`, and `Name( )` with no args; 202 a **function application** does not — `f(a, b)`; a **tuple** is `<a, b>`. Verified 203 against the corpus. 204 - **Escaping**: inside a record label, `< > { } |` each get a leading backslash; 205 everything else (quotes, `~ $ ^ * ⊕`, spaces) is literal. 206 - **Line wrapping FORMAT** (byte-exact). When a group breaks, physical lines are 207 separated by `\l` (with a trailing `\l`), and each continuation line is indented 208 with a ` ` run **equal to the column of the broken group's first element** 209 (just after `( ` for a fact, after `<` for a tuple, after `[` for an action 210 list). Verified across 188 192 wrapped cells. 211 - **Line wrapping DECISION — RESOLVED (Session 4): a fixed-width paragraph fill, 212 width = 87 columns, measured per fact from column 0.** The Round-3 claim ("first 213 line 5..87, not a flat width, not derivable") is **refuted**: it *is* a flat 214 width. Established by driving crafted single-node theories 215 (`Out(<'a01', …, 'aN'>)`, `Out(<'aa…a', 'y'>)`) through the live server and 216 sweeping the term width one column at a time: 217 - **W = 87 absolute, functor-invariant.** A fact stays on one line iff its flat 218 rendering is ≤ 87 columns; at 88 it breaks. Tested with functor names of length 219 2, 3, 6, 10 (`Ba`,`Out`,`Fact12`,`Factabcdef`): the boundary was **always** at 220 flat width 87 fits / 88 breaks, so the budget is counted from the functor's own 221 column 0, *including* the functor name (a longer name simply fits fewer args). 222 Corpus-consistent: 99.2 % of 190 044 physical lines have fact-content ≤ 87; the 223 residual are single unbreakable atoms wider than 87 (which overflow verbatim). 224 - **Greedy paragraph fill.** When a group overflows, elements pack left-to-right; 225 the separator `, ` trails the element it follows and stays on the line, and the 226 next element wraps when it would pass column 87. Continuation lines use the 227 §3f indent. Example `Out(<'a01'…'a12'>)` (flat 91): line 0 = 228 `Out( <'a01', … 'a11', ` (eleven elements + trailing `, `, ending col 83), 229 `'a12'>` on the next line, then the fact's `)` on its own line. 230 - **Delimiter peel — BYTE-IMPLEMENTED (Session 5).** Re-probed the boundary with 231 fresh single-node sweeps (`E10..E14`, `W69..W74`, ports 3210): a **tuple's `>`** 232 stays with the last element iff it fits (`Out( <'aaa(72)', 'y'>` │ `)`), else it 233 peels onto its own line at the tuple's **`<` column** (`W74`: `…'y'` │ `<5sp>>` 234 │ `)`); a **fact's `)` ALWAYS** peels onto its own line at **col 0** once the 235 fact wraps — even when it would fit (`E12`: `'a12'>` │ `)`) — because the padded 236 ` )` space is the break. An **info action-list `]`** stays attached to its last 237 action. An unbreakable atom wider than the budget overflows verbatim; only the 238 trailing delimiters wrap. 239 - **Continuation lookahead — RESOLVED (subsumed).** The old "first line 11, 240 continuation 12" claim is a mis-read: the continuation packs **greedily to the 241 same width 87** as the first line (`E13`→2, `E14`→3 elements on the second line, 242 each ending well under 87). There is no ±1 combinator lookahead to reproduce. 243 - **Info action-list = vertical `sep`, not fill.** An overflowing info cell puts 244 **one action per line** (corpus `6738eb64…`: 4 actions → 4 lines, short actions 245 included), `]` on the last; whereas a tuple / fact-arg list uses the greedy 246 fill. Two distinct combinators, both byte-implemented. 247 - **`graph-clean::render::wrap_cell`** implements all of the above (`layout_fact` 248 fill+`)`-peel, `layout_tuple` fill+`>`-peel, `layout_info` vertical `sep`, 249 `run_layout` greedy fill, `split_top_commas`), wired into 250 `generate::build_record`; byte-verified against the 7 captured probe fixtures 251 (`wrap_E11..E14`, `wrap_W71/W72/W74`). 252 - **NEW RESIDUAL — the wrap TRIGGER is accumulated-column (Wadler `group`/`fits`).** 253 `wrap_cell` decides to wrap on the cell's **own flat width** > 87, which is 254 correct for a cell early on its record line (all probes/fixtures). But a cell 255 deep on a wide record line wraps **earlier**: measured over 61 821 corpus 256 wrapped records, physical line-0 width ranges 19..277 (no fixed absolute page 257 width), and a 21-char fact `Y_counter( pid.1, t )` at line-start peels its `)` 258 while an identical-args instance late on a broken line stays flat — because 259 tamarin's `fits` measures the flat rendering **plus the rest of the record 260 line** against the remaining width. Reproducing that trigger needs the exact 261 record document tree (the whole label as one `group`/`nest`/`line` Doc); the 262 width (87), fill packing, peel columns, and the fill-vs-sep split are pinned. 263 264 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 265 ## 4. Clustering / simplification (§ priority 3) 266 267 **Trigger rule (byte-verified over all 14705 graphs, 0 violations):** 268 > The COMPACT clustered rendering (§2b, with `subgraph "cluster_…"` blocks) is 269 > used **iff the graph contains at least one node with `role` ≠ `"Undefined"`.** 270 > Otherwise the SIMPLE header (§2a) is used. 271 272 Non-Undefined roles arise from process/SAPIC agents (e.g. `Initiator`, 273 `Responder`, `server`, `Tag`, `v`). Pure multiset-rewriting theories emit the 274 simple header everywhere; role-annotated theories emit the compact header on 275 every non-empty graph (empty graphs have no role nodes ⇒ simple). 276 277 ### Cluster block 278 ``` 279 subgraph "cluster_<Role>_Session_<k>" { 280 nodesep="0.6"; 281 ranksep="0.6"; 282 label="<Role>_Session_<k>"; 283 style="filled"; 284 color="#RRGGBBAA"; 285 penwidth="2"; 286 fillcolor="#RRGGBBAA"; 287 overlap="false"; 288 sep="4"; 289 n<ID>[shape="record",label="…",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="white",role="<Role>"]; 290 291 } 292 ``` 293 - Cluster name = `cluster_<clusterlabel>`; `label` = the clusterlabel 294 (`<Role>_Session_<k>`). Nodes grouped by (role, session instance). 295 - `color`/`fillcolor` are an 8-hex ARGB (alpha `4C`, ~30%) hashed from the 296 cluster; node `fillcolor` is the saturated per-role color (live SAPIC probe 297 `cluster_Process_Session_1` had node `fillcolor="#ffffff"`, `fontcolor="black"` 298 — so the node fill is not always saturated). Exact hash **[GAP]**. 299 300 ### Cluster EMISSION ORDER (§ Round-5 item 1) — byte-verified over 1457 corpus files 301 The top-level statement order of a clustered graph is **always** 302 `[free nodes] [clusters] [edges] [rankblock/legend] [invis edges]`: 0 files have a 303 top-level node after a cluster, 0 clusters after an edge. **Clusters contain only 304 records** (50 453 record cells, 0 ellipse/edge). A cluster's records occupy a 305 **contiguous** id range and clusters are ordered by increasing id (first-appearance 306 == id order). Since id order == emission order (§3e), the global id counter runs 307 over the free (non-role) nodes first — giving them the low ids — then over the 308 clustered records grouped by cluster. `graph-clean::generate` reproduces this: 309 Pass 1 allocates ids in `System.nodes` order, routing each role-record's statement 310 into its `cluster(label,color)` bucket and everything else to the free top level, 311 then emits free nodes, then cluster blocks in first-appearance order, then edges, 312 then the legend. Byte-exact on a live SAPIC single-cluster graph 313 (`cluster_process.dot`) and the multi-cluster corpus payload 314 `79c16911ad179d51.dot` (4 free ellipses + 1 cluster + 2 deduction edges). 315 316 ### What "simplification" means here 317 There is no exposed simplification *level*; the corpus reflects one fixed 318 rendering pipeline. The observable "simplification" is the fixed compression the 319 server applies before rendering: intruder-knowledge (`!KU`) nodes are drawn as 320 gray ellipses; single-premise/conclusion structural chains are drawn with bold 321 weight-10 edges; role nodes are packed into per-session clusters (compact mode). 322 Reproducing the *content* of that compression from a raw constraint system 323 requires the solver and is **[GAP]** — out of scope for a rendering crate. 324 325 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 326 ## 5. Node abbreviation (§ priority 2) 327 328 When a graph is drawn, sufficiently complex sub-terms are replaced by short 329 NAMES and listed in a legend node. Observed from 113 924 legend rows. 330 331 ### 5a. Legend node 332 A `plain` node with an HTML-like label, placed in a sink-rank block: 333 ``` 334 { 335 rank="sink"; 336 n<ID>[shape="plain",label=<<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1"><TR>…row0…</TR> 337 <65 spaces><TR>…row1…</TR> 338 … 339 <65 spaces><TR>…rowN…</TR></TABLE>>]; 340 341 } 342 ``` 343 - Each row: 344 `<TR><TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP"><FONT COLOR="#000000">NAME</FONT></TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">=</TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">EXPANSION</TD></TR>` 345 (single spaces between `</TD>` and the next `<TD>`). 346 - The first `<TR>` is inline after the `<TABLE …>` tag; every continuation `<TR>` 347 is on its own line indented by **65 spaces = the byte length of the 348 `<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1">` opening 349 tag** (graphviz hang-indent of the table's children; independent of node id). 350 - EXPANSION is HTML-escaped: `<`→`<`, `>`→`>`, `&`→`&`. Single quotes 351 and `* ^ ~ $ ⊕` are literal. 352 - The legend node is wired to the rest with `style="invis"` edges (from a couple 353 of real nodes to the legend), emitted AFTER the sink block. 354 355 ### 5b. Naming scheme (byte-verified rule) 356 `NAME = <PREFIX><n>`, n = 1,2,3,… per prefix. 357 `PREFIX` = the first **two alphabetic characters, uppercased**, of the ROOT 358 symbol's *name* (one letter if the name has only one). The root symbol name is: 359 - function application `f(…)` ⇒ `f` (`sign`→SI, `senc`→SE, `hash`→HA, 360 `h`/`h1`/`h2`→H, `KDF`/`kdf1`→KD, `pk`→PK, `aenc`/`aead`→AE, `pmult`→PM, …) 361 - public constant `'c'` ⇒ `c` (`'uninitialized'`→UN, `'F_status'`→FS, 362 `'ho_req_ack'`→HO, `'no_message_state'`→NO — non-letters skipped: F,s→FS) 363 - variable `~v` / `$v` / `v` ⇒ `v` (`~AMF_UE_NGAP_ID`→AM, `~cid_N26`→CI, 364 `~gNB_UE_ID`→GN, `~mid…`→MI, `commitmsg`→CO, `StateChannel`→ST) 365 - infix operator, mapped to its function name then the letter rule: 366 `^`→exp→**EX**, `*`→mult→**MU**, `++`→union→**UN**, `⊕`→xor→**XO**. 367 368 Abbreviations NEST: an inner abbreviated term is referenced by name inside an 369 outer expansion (`KD1='KDF(EX1)'`, `MA1='mac(EX1, SI1)'`, `EX1="'g'^MU1"`, 370 `H1="h(<…, EX2, EX3, EX1, …>)"`). So naming is applied bottom-up. 371 372 Per-prefix numbering is by tamarin's canonical term order, NOT by first 373 appearance in the drawing (observed: `In(EX2)` is the first occurrence yet gets 374 `EX2`, and larger/deeper terms get the lower numbers within a prefix). The exact 375 tie-break total order is **[GAP]** (needs the solver's term ordering); the crate 376 assigns per-prefix numbers over a defined order and matches whenever that order 377 is unambiguous. 378 379 ### 5c. Selection — WHICH sub-terms get abbreviated **[RESOLVED — see REPORT2.md]** 380 381 **Rule (three necessary gates; each confirmed by a controlled black-box probe):** 382 > A sub-term `t` is abbreviated **iff** 383 > 1. `renderLen(t) ≥ 10` (Unicode-scalar length of the fully-expanded surface 384 > rendering, *including* `'…'` quotes and `~`/`$` sigils), AND 385 > 2. `occ(t) ≥ 2` (occurrences over the whole constraint system — graph node 386 > facts **and** the sequent's goals/formulas — edge-shared messages count 387 > once), AND 388 > 3. `t` is **not** a tuple `<…>`. 389 > Naming is bottom-up: nested eligible sub-terms are named first 390 > (`LO1='longarg123'`, then `H1='h(LO1)'`). 391 392 This is a rendered **length** gate, not the *size* or *occurrence* the earlier 393 note guessed at — which is why it looked context-free-yet-unexplained. It 394 cleanly settles the old counter-observations: `sign(<…,'g'^~ekR>,~ltkI)` is ≥10 395 chars (abbreviated); `KDF(z)` is 6 chars (never abbreviated, whatever its count); 396 `mac(z,SI1)` and `'g'^~lkR` are below the length/occurrence thresholds in their 397 graphs. `'g'^~lkR` vs `'g'^~lkR.1` differ because the `.1` copy reaches occ 2 in 398 the system while the other does not. 399 400 **Corpus-exactness (necessary direction):** across all **97 538** legend 401 expansions in **11 564** graphs, the minimum rendered length is exactly **10** 402 (0 below) and **0** entries are top-level tuples. Among terms drawn ≥2× in a 403 graph, **0** abbreviated terms violate len≥10 ∧ ¬tuple. Abbreviated terms with 404 graph-occurrence 1 (7 800) all reach ≥2 once the sequent is counted. 405 406 **Controlled probes (crafted theories, live server; see QUERIES.log):** 407 - `'12345678'` (10 chars, ×2) abbreviated; `'1234567'` (9, ×2) not ⇒ boundary = 10. 408 - `'ABCDEFGH'`/`'onceonly12'` (×1) not abbreviated; `'twicelong1'` (×2) is; 409 a 42-char constant (×1) not abbreviated ⇒ occ ≥ 2 is a hard gate at any length. 410 - `<'aa','bb','cc'>` (18 chars, ×2) not abbreviated while sibling `h('longarg123')` 411 (×2) is ⇒ tuple exclusion + bottom-up nesting. 412 413 **Residual (sufficiency):** for **AC/DH-operator** sub-terms (`^ * ⊕ ++ inv em 414 hp pmult`) tamarin counts occurrences over the *normalised* (AC-flattened, 415 DH-normal-form) term, not the surface rendering, so the same term can be 416 abbreviated in one proof state and not another (`em(hp($A),hp($B))` inline in 417 state `56eeb42d`, `EM1` in `54eb2e2c`). 93 % of apparent exceptions are such 418 AC/DH cases; the rest are validation artifacts from expanding shared 419 abbreviations before counting. Outside AC/DH terms the rule is exact. 420 421 The crate `abbrev::select` implements this rule (`MIN_ABBREV_LEN=10`, 422 `MIN_ABBREV_OCC=2`, tuple-excluded, bottom-up); tests reproduce every probe. 423 Byte-parity of the selection set is claimed for non-AC/DH terms and gated by the 424 caller supplying the full-system term multiset. 425 426 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 427 ## 6. System → graph mapping (§ Round-3 item 4) 428 429 From paired `main/proof` sequents and `interactive-graph-def` graphs, the 430 structural mapping a proof state yields (each point traces to §3d/§3f and the 431 content census; the *content* — which nodes/edges exist — is a solver GAP): 432 - a **rule instance** `#t : Rule[actions]` → a `record` node with groups 433 `{premises}|{info}|{conclusions}` (empty prem/concl dropped, info always kept); 434 - an **intruder-knowledge fact** → a gray `!KU( m ) @ #t` ellipse; 435 - a **protocol action/event** → a darkblue `Fact @ #t` ellipse; 436 - a **compressed intruder rule** → an uncolored `#t : rule` ellipse (§4 compression); 437 - an **unresolved node** referenced by an open premise → an `invtrapezium` 438 `(#var, idx)` (§3d); 439 - **edges** connect conclusion-port → premise-port (structural bold w10 [gray50]), 440 intruder deduction (red dashed), message (gray30), temporal order 441 (blue3/black dashed), etc. — the finite §3c vocabulary; 442 - ids from §3e; header from §4 (role); legend from §5. 443 444 `graph-clean::generate` implements this over an independent input model 445 (`System`/`GraphNode`/`RuleInstance`/`SysEdge`), taking node/edge lists, colors, 446 and cell text as INPUTS. `tests/generate_tests.rs` reproduces a live source-case 447 graph (`nsl_invtrap.dot`) **byte-exact** end-to-end. 448 449 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 450 ## 7. Simplification / abbreviation options (§ Round-3 item 5) 451 452 The UI's "Graph simplification off/L1/L2/L3" menu and abbreviation toggle are 453 cookie-driven and appended by the JS as graph-URL **query params** (mined from 454 `/static/js/tamarin-prover-ui.js`, confirmed by live diffs — QUERIES.log): 455 456 | param | UI sends when | observed effect | 457 |--------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------| 458 | `simplification=N` | always (N=level, default 2) | **inert** — the *number* changes nothing (see below) | 459 | `uncompact=` | level 0 only | node COMPACTION off (lone intruder rule → full record vs ellipse) | 460 | `uncompress=` | level 0 only | system COMPRESSION off (Fresh sources + intruder rules re-expand to records) | 461 | `unabbreviate=` | abbreviate cookie off | abbreviation off (terms inlined, legend block omitted) | 462 | `no-auto-sources=` | auto-sources cookie off | disables auto-source precomputation | 463 | `clustering=true` | clustering cookie on | forces role clustering | 464 465 ### 7a. The L1/L2/L3 distinction — RESOLVED (channel-verified negative, Session 4) 466 467 The Round-3 GAP ("simplification 1/2/3 produced byte-identical graphs") is **not a 468 probing artifact and not a hidden session channel** — it is the genuine server 469 behavior: **there is no L1/L2/L3 distinction; the `simplification=N` *number* is 470 inert for every N.** Established under the session-state lens the follow-up 471 demanded: 472 473 - **Channel.** The served JS (`tamarin-prover-ui.js`, lines 78–100 / 476–500) 474 reads a *client-side* `simplification` cookie (default 2) and converts it into 475 the graph-URL query param; at level 0 it *additionally* appends `uncompact=` + 476 `uncompress=`. Levels 1/2/3 send **only** `simplification=N`. The server emits 477 **no `Set-Cookie` on any response** (root, case-graph, main/cases json, proof 478 method) — there is *no server-side session state*; the param is the only channel. 479 - **Change verified before diffing.** Driving the *flags* through the URL param 480 demonstrably changes the graph (e.g. rich NSLPK3 case `refined/6/5`: default 481 5 668 B → `uncompact=&uncompress=` 7 792 B), and the result is byte-identical 482 whether or not a matching cookie is also sent. So the URL param **is** honored. 483 - **The number is inert.** On that 17-node case graph and on a **39-node, 27 KB** 484 DH/bilinear proof-state graph (`eurosp19-eccDAA` analysed, 485 `proof/functional_correctness/_`), every probe of `simplification` ∈ 486 {0,1,2,3,4,5,10,99} — via URL param, via cookie, and via both together, and 487 crossed with every flag combination — collapses to a **single** md5. `flags_L0` 488 … `flags_L99` are byte-identical. (Second theory NAXOS_eCK case `refined/4/1` 489 reproduces this.) 490 - **Mechanism.** The UI's four-item menu (`for i<4`: off / L1 / L2 / L3) dresses up 491 a *binary*: "off" (level 0) = the UI adds the two flags; "L1/L2/L3" = no flags, 492 and the server never consumes the level integer. The real transforms are the 493 independent boolean flags, each with a distinct, measured effect on the 39-node 494 graph: `uncompact=` 39 nodes→richer records (27 361→30 880 B); `uncompress=` 495 39→**74** nodes (re-expands compressed chains, 31 407 B); `unabbreviate=` drops 496 the legend node (39→38) and inlines terms (36 690 B, 0 legend rows); 497 level 0 (`uncompact`+`uncompress`) = 74 nodes, byte-identical to the two flags 498 sent explicitly. 499 500 `graph-clean::options::Options` models the knobs and reproduces the exact query 501 string; `simplification=N` is carried but is documented inert. The abbreviation 502 on/off transform is applied directly (drop legend / inline). Remaining **GAP** 503 here: the *content* of compress/compact (a solver transform — the caller supplies 504 the already-(un)compressed node set). 505 506 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 507 ## 8. What the crate reproduces vs. gaps 508 509 Reproduced & byte-tested against captured/live payloads: 510 - DOT document assembly: both headers, block/whitespace rule, node lines 511 (record/ellipse/plain/**invtrapezium**), edge lines with ports & attrs, cluster 512 subgraphs, legend sink-block + invis edges, empty graph. Round-trip reproduces 513 **12 022/12 022** corpus payloads byte-exact (`tests/roundtrip.rs`). 514 - **Node-id/port allocation** (§3e): `NodeIdAllocator`; validated 12 022/12 022 via 515 the crate model (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`). 516 - **Record-cell rendering** (§3f): fact/function/tuple spacing, info-cell shape, 517 group-drop rule, record escaping, and the wrap **DECISION + FORMAT + PEEL** — 518 `wrap_cell` (`FILL_WIDTH=87`, greedy `fillSep` for fact-arg/tuple lists, vertical 519 `sep` for info action lists, tuple-`>` peel to the `<` column, fact-`)` peel to 520 col 0), wired into generation and byte-verified against 7 live boundary probes. 521 - **System → graph GENERATION** (§4, §6): `generate` over the independent `System` 522 model — free/ellipse/record/invtrapezium nodes, **role clusters** (`subgraph 523 cluster_…`, free-then-cluster id/emission order), the **`#last`/bare-timepoint** 524 ellipse, the finite edge vocabulary, id/port allocation, and the wired record-cell 525 wrap. Byte-exact on live + corpus fixtures (invtrap, single/multi cluster, 526 `#last`, wrap sweep) in `tests/generate_tests.rs`. 527 - **Pre-rendered-cell interop** (§ round 5): `RawRule` / `GraphNode::RawRule` accept 528 already-rendered cell strings and run them through the same wrap/escape pipeline, 529 byte-identical to the Term-based path (`raw_rule_matches_term_path_byte_exact`). 530 - **Simplification/abbreviation options** (§7): `Options` query-string model; the 531 **L1/L2/L3 level number is proven inert** (§7a) — channel-verified negative. 532 - Abbreviation naming, numbering, legend HTML (65-space indent), and the SELECTION 533 rule (§5c, REPORT2.md), plus the cluster/compact trigger (§4). 534 535 Documented gaps (need the GPL solver or an unavailable backend): 536 - JSON graph backend format (unavailable / not in corpus). 537 - **Record-cell wrap TRIGGER** (§3f): the width (87), fill packing, delimiter peel, 538 and the fill-vs-`sep` split are byte-implemented; the remaining residual is *when* 539 a cell wraps — tamarin's Wadler `group`/`fits` wraps a cell earlier when preceding 540 cells push its absolute column high (flat width alone can be < 87), which needs the 541 exact record document tree. `wrap_cell` triggers on the cell's own flat width > 87 542 (exact for cells early on their record line). 543 - **compress/compact content** (§4, §6): which nodes/edges a raw constraint system 544 yields — a solver transform. (The L1/L2/L3 level distinction is no longer a gap: 545 it is proven non-existent, §7a.) 546 - Per-rule/per-cluster **color hashes** (§3a, §4); the `trapezium` dual (§3d, unobserved). 547 - Abbreviation of AC/DH sub-terms (§5c residual, normalised-form occurrence). 548 - Canonical per-prefix numbering tie-break (§5b); empty-prefix start index. 549

1	# Clean-room task: constraint-system graph rendering (dot + JSON)
2	
3	Build, from BLACK-BOX BEHAVIOR ONLY, a standalone crate `graph-clean` in
4	workspace/ that reproduces the graph outputs of the tamarin-prover web UI:
5	the graphviz DOT text and/or JSON graph payloads served for a proof state,
6	including node ABBREVIATION and graph SIMPLIFICATION/clustering behavior.
7	
8	## Oracles
9	- oracle/captured_responses/ -> 81 crawl manifests of the Haskell web UI
10	  (JSON: url->response for whole theories; explore the format yourself).
11	  These are captured program OUTPUT — your primary corpus.
12	- oracle/hs_server.sh start <file.spthy> <port> serves a live instance for
13	  targeted probing (curl). Example inputs: ../wellformedness/oracle/examples/.
14	
15	## Deliverables
16	1. workspace/BEHAVIOR.md — precise observed spec: graph payload format, node/
17	   edge structure, how node labels are abbreviated (rules, thresholds), what
18	   simplification levels do, cluster structure, exact DOT/JSON syntax.
19	2. `graph-clean` implementing the transforms over YOUR OWN graph data model
20	   (design it from observed payloads; an integration adapter will be written
21	   later by others). Priority: abbreviation + simplification transforms with
22	   tests against captured payload pairs.
23	3. workspace/QUERIES.log as per protocol.
24	Dependencies: std + serde/serde_json only.
25

122	  solving).
123	- Simplification-level diffs on NAXOS refined/1/2 and largest NSLPK3 case:
124	  default == simplification=1 == 2 == 3 (byte-identical on every probed graph;
125	  L1/L2/L3 distinction UNobserved => GAP). Level-0 (uncompact+uncompress) EXPANDS
126	  the graph: Fresh sources + intruder rules become full records; !KU ellipses and
127	  isend/coerce ellipses re-expand. unabbreviate inlines terms and drops the legend
128	  sink-block. Isolated uncompact vs uncompress each partially expand.
129	- SHAPE hunt: invtrapezium `(#i, N)` observed live (a `(#nodevar, premiseIndex)`
130	  placeholder for an unresolved node referenced by an open premise; fed by the
131	  conclusion that must satisfy it) — in default AND uncompressed case graphs of
132	  BOTH NAXOS and NSLPK3. Plain `trapezium` NOT observed in any probe (all case
133	  graphs, compressed+uncompressed, 2 theories) nor the corpus — recorded as
134	  unobserved-but-spec-named. Walked eCK_key_secrecy proof steps (method/1) — small
135	  ellipse-only states, no new shapes.
136	- Saved fixtures: nsl_invtrap.dot, invtrap_compressed.dot (=NAXOS default),
137	  invtrap_raw.dot (=level-0 raw). Servers stopped after probing.
138	
139	## Session 4 — close L1/L2/L3 gap + re-attempt wrap DECISION (session-state lens)
140	
141	### Channel investigation (served JS + response headers, all HTTP-observed OUTPUT)
142	- Fetched /static/js/tamarin-prover-ui.js from the black-box binary. The graph
143	  URL params are built from CLIENT-SIDE cookies (jquery-cookie): handleJson (lines
144	  78–100) appends `simplification=<cookie>` always, plus `uncompact=`+`uncompress=`
145	  ONLY when the cookie==0, plus `unabbreviate=`/`no-auto-sources=` per their
146	  cookies. Level-button loop is `for i<4` (off/L1/L2/L3), default cookie=2. No
147	  AJAX/POST sets a graph preference server-side; the POSTs are lemma editing only.
148	- `curl -i` on root, case-graph, main/cases json, and a proof-method request:
149	  **no Set-Cookie on ANY response** ⇒ no server-side session state; the URL query
150	  param is the sole channel. (This is what the follow-up's "session-state lens"
151	  warned to check; the channel is verified to be the URL param, not a cookie.)
152	
153	### Live level probes (serve.sh wrapper, --port= form; ports 3200–3203)
154	- NSLPK3 :3200, richest source-case graph `refined/6/5` (17 nodes, 5 668 B). Matrix
155	  over `simplification` ∈ {0,1,2,3} × {URL param, cookie `-b`, both}: ALL byte-
156	  identical to baseline (one md5). Adding `uncompact=&uncompress=` (the real level-0
157	  flags) DID change it (7 792 B) and was identical with/without a cookie ⇒ URL param
158	  honored, change verified before diffing. Level number ∈ {0,1,2,3,4,5,10,99}
159	  crossed with 8 flag combos: within every combo, L1/L2/L3 collapse to 1 md5;
160	  `flags_L0`…`flags_L99` all one md5. Individual flag effects distinct (uncompact
161	  6 496, uncompress 6 326, unabbreviate 4 695, clustering 6 388 B).
162	- NAXOS_eCK :3201 case `refined/4/1` (2 912 B): L0=L1=L2=L3 identical, L0+flags
163	  differs. Reproduces the negative on a 2nd theory.

165	  `proof/functional_correctness/_` = **39 nodes, 27 361 B**. All level probes
166	  (url/cookie/both, {0,1,2,3,4,10}) → 1 md5; no Set-Cookie. Flag effects measured:
167	  uncompact 30 880 B (records richer, still 39), uncompress **74 nodes** 31 407 B,
168	  unabbreviate 38 nodes/36 690 B/0 legend rows, uncompact+uncompress==level0 (74
169	  nodes). ⇒ CONCLUSION: no L1/L2/L3 distinction; the level number is inert; "off"
170	  (L0) = the UI adding two flags. GAP closed (rigorous negative + mechanism).
171

173	- Route discovery: autoprove via GET /thy/trace/1/autoprove/idfs/0/False/proof/<L>
174	  (returns redirect to a new theory index); proof-state graph at

176	  at line 922 (dotsrc + "?" + query_string) with no width/wrap parameter; layout
177	  east-size/west-size cookies are client-only (never sent) ⇒ wrap has NO channel;
178	  it is a fixed server-side pretty-printer, re-attacked by input control.
179	- Two sweeps solved live (ports 3220–3236, driver.py/gen_sweep.py):
180	  (a) `Out(<'a01',…,'aN'>)` N=1..29 — pure fill of 5-col elements;
181	  (b) `Out(<'aa…a'(P), 'y'>)` P=60..95 — one-column flat-width sweep.
182	  Findings: single-line iff flat width ≤ **87**; 88 breaks. First line packs 11
183	  elements + trailing ", " (ends col 83); continuation packs 12 (fsep lookahead).
184	  Closing `)` peels to col 0; tuple `>` peels to the `<` column; long unbreakable
185	  atoms overflow verbatim.

208	  (100410), `Action(..)@#t` (3586), there are BARE TIMEPOINT ellipses `#<var>` —
209	  `#last` (107), `#i` (1328), `#decrypt` (790), `#t0..#t6`, `#j`, `#j.1`. `#last`
210	  is uncolored `n<ID>[label="#last",shape="ellipse"]`, the target of a
211	  `color="black",style="dashed"` before-edge (fixture 24a119958f784d43.dot).
212	- WRAP measurement (measure_break.py): physical line-0 visual width of 61821
213	  wrapped records ranges 19..277 (median 95) ⇒ NO fixed page width in absolute
214	  columns; the accumulated-column effect is real. Y_counter probe (4c6f68b4...):
215	  a 21-char fact `Y_counter( pid.1, t )` at line-start peels its `)` while an
216	  IDENTICAL-args longer instance late on a broken line does NOT ⇒ wrap trigger is
217	  Wadler `group`/`fits` (flat + rest-of-line vs remaining width), not per-cell-87.
218	- INFO action-list wrap style: one action PER LINE (vertical `sep`) even when short
219	  (6738eb64..: 4 actions, 4 lines; `Decrypt( m )` alone on its line), `]` attached
220	  to the last action. Contrast tuple/fact args = greedy fill (`fillSep`). b5a8773..
221	  info cell wraps at flat 52 (< 87) ⇒ info-cell trigger is also accumulated-column.
222	
223	### Live server probes (scratchpad/serve.sh, --port= form; ports 3210–3213)
224	- wrap.spthy :3210 — 11 one-rule theories, each `rule EN/WP: [Fr(~s)] --[X()]->
225	  [Out(<..>)]` + exists-trace lemma. Autoproved each via GET autoprove/idfs/0/False
226	  /proof/l_X (returns {"redirect":"/thy/trace/<idx>/..."}), fetched

228	  11 elems fit one line (col 83 after trailing ", "), 'a12' wraps to col-6 indent;
229	  continuation packs GREEDILY (E13→2, E14→3 on the second line) to the SAME width —
230	  the earlier "+1 lookahead" is subsumed. Width sweep W69..W74 (`<'a…a'(P),'y'>`):
231	  P=71 flat 87 fits, P=72 flat 88 breaks. Peel: tuple `>` stays with last element
232	  iff it fits (W72), else peels to the `<` column on its own line (W74, indent 5);
233	  fact `)` ALWAYS peels to col 0 once the fact wraps (E12 peels `)` even though it
234	  would fit). Captured 7 fixtures (wrap_E11..E14, W71/W72/W74).
235	- ind.spthy :3211 — autoprove ord [use_induction]; the lemma discharged

238	- decwrap-destr-manual.spthy (SAPIC) :3212 — autoprove can_create_key; captured a
239	  live cluster graph `cluster_Process_Session_1` (role="Process",
240	  node fillcolor="#ffffff", fontcolor="black", cluster ARGB "#36A5D84C"). Saved

242	- NSLPK3.spthy :3213 — scanned interactive-graph-def/cases/{raw,refined}/i/j

244	  `n5:n2 -> n6` structural gray50 edge ⇒ invtrapezium = "conclusion feeding an

247	  generic `GraphNode::Shaped{label,shape,color}` can emit it if a caller supplies one.
248	- All four servers stopped after probing (serve.sh stop 3210..3213).
249	
250	### Implemented (graph-clean)
251	- render::wrap_cell — byte-exact wrap+peel: layout_fact (fill args / recurse into a
252	  sole tuple / peel `)` to col 0), layout_tuple (fill + `>` peel to `<`-col),

254	  Wired into generate::build_record for premise/info/conclusion cells.

257	  GraphNode::Shaped (generic shape escape hatch, incl. trapezium dual); RawRule +

1	#!/usr/bin/env bash
2	# Black-box oracle: serve a .spthy in the HS interactive web UI.
3	# Usage: hs_server.sh start <file.spthy> <port>   (then curl http://127.0.0.1:<port>/...)
4	#        hs_server.sh stop <port>
5	export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
6	case "$1" in
7	  start) d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/"; nohup "/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover" interactive "$d" --port "$3" >/tmp/hs_server_$3.log 2>&1 & echo $! > /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid; sleep 4; echo "up on $3 (pid $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$3.pid))";;
8	  stop) kill $(cat /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid) 2>/dev/null; rm -f /tmp/hs_server_$2.pid; echo stopped;;
9	esac
10

1	# REPORT2 — the abbreviation SELECTION rule (BEHAVIOR.md §5c gap)
2	
3	Clean-room continuation of `graphdot`. Everything below traces to observed
4	oracle output: the captured DOT corpus (`oracle/dot_corpus/`, 12 022 unique
5	payloads), the captured crawl manifests (`oracle/captured_responses/`, 81
6	theories; the `main/proof` json giving the full sequent paired 1:1 with each
7	`interactive-graph-def` DOT), and controlled probes of the black-box server
8	(`hs_server` binary, `--port=` form). No tamarin-prover source was read.
9	
10	## Result in one line
11	
12	> A sub-term `t` of a constraint system is abbreviated in the graph **iff**
13	> (1) its rendered length >= **10**, (2) it occurs >= **2** times in the system,
14	> and (3) it is not a tuple `<...>`. Naming is bottom-up (nested eligible
15	> sub-terms are named first).
16	
17	The prior named puzzle -- why `sign(...)` at 3 occurrences is abbreviated while
18	`KDF(z)` at 4 is not -- is resolved: the gate is rendered **string length**, not
19	structural size or occurrence count. `sign(<...>,...)` is >=10 chars; `KDF(z)`
20	is 6. The earlier investigator measured *size* and *occurrence* and so could not
21	separate them.
22	
23	## Evidence
24	
25	### (1) Length >= 10 -- necessary, corpus-exact
26	Over **all 97 538** legend expansions in **11 564** graphs (every legend in the
27	corpus), the minimum fully-expanded rendered length is **exactly 10**; **zero**
28	are shorter. Histogram floor: len10=7397, len11=3071, len12=3776, ... nothing at
29	5-9. Length is measured on tamarin's own rendering *including* surface
30	decorations -- quotes, `~`/`$` sigils (a bare `pk(PL1)` is only 7 chars but is
31	abbreviated because it *expands* to >=10; the decision is on the full expansion).
32	
33	Controlled probe (`LenTest.spthy`): a rule whose one node emits
34	`Keep(~x, '12345678', '12345678', '1234567', '1234567')`. Observed graph:
35	`Keep( ~x, 2, 2, '1234567', '1234567' )` with legend `2 = '12345678'`.
36	`'12345678'` = 10 chars -> abbreviated; `'1234567'` = 9 chars, **same occurrence
37	count** -> not. The boundary is exactly 10.
38	
39	### (2) Occurrence >= 2 -- necessary, hard gate independent of length
40	Structural per-graph analysis of 5 725 graphs (156 602 distinct candidate
41	sub-terms): among terms drawn >=2 times, **every** abbreviated term satisfies
42	len>=10 AND not-tuple -- **0** counterexamples. The 7 800 abbreviated terms the
43	graph-only occurrence "misses" all have graph-occurrence 1: they occur once in
44	the *drawn* graph but >=2 in the full system (the sequent's goals/formulas). This
45	is why terms such as `SI3 = sign(~y, k)` appear in a legend while being drawn
46	**nowhere** in the graph -- the abbreviation universe is the whole constraint
47	system, not the drawn nodes. Pairing each DOT with its `main/proof` json sequent
48	recovers those second occurrences.
49	
50	Controlled probe (`OccTest.spthy`): `'ABCDEFGH'` (10 chars, x1) and
51	`'onceonly12'` (12 chars, x1) are **not** abbreviated; `'twicelong1'` (12, x2)
52	**is** (`TW1`). Probe (`LongOnce.spthy`): a **42-char** constant appearing once
53	is **not** abbreviated, while a 12-char constant appearing twice is. So there is
54	no "very long => abbreviate at 1 occurrence" branch -- occ >= 2 is a hard gate.
55	
56	### (3) Tuples are never abbreviated -- necessary, corpus-exact
57	**0 of 97 538** legend expansions is a top-level tuple. Only function
58	applications, exponentiations, AC-operator terms (`* (+) ++`), and atoms (long
59	constants/variables) are abbreviated. Controlled probe (`TupTest.spthy`):
60	`<'aa','bb','cc'>` (18 chars, x2) stays inline, while the sibling
61	`h('longarg123')` (x2) is abbreviated to `H1` with `LO1 = 'longarg123'`,
62	`H1 = h(LO1)` -- confirming the tuple exclusion and **bottom-up nesting** (inner
63	`'longarg123'` named before the enclosing `h(...)`), and the prefix rule
64	(`lo`->LO, `h`->H).
65	
66	## Accuracy and the residual
67	
68	The three gates are **necessary** with 0 corpus counterexamples (length, tuple)
69	/ 0 counterexamples among drawn->=2 terms (occurrence). For **sufficiency** --
70	is every (len>=10 AND occ>=2 AND not-tuple) term abbreviated? -- the controlled
71	probes say yes for ordinary terms, and the corpus agrees except for one
72	characterized residual:
73	
74	* **93.3%** of apparent sufficiency-exceptions occur in graphs using an **AC/DH
75	  operator** (`^ * (+) ++ inv em hp pmult`). Example: in state `56eeb42d`,
76	  `em(hp($A), hp($B))` (18 chars, drawn 7x) is *not* abbreviated, yet in a later
77	  state `54eb2e2c` the *same* term *is* (`EM1`). The difference is how DH
78	  exponentiation groups the term: tamarin counts occurrences over the
79	  **normalised** (AC-flattened, DH-normal-form) term, which a surface count does
80	  not model. This is solver-normalisation-dependent and cannot be derived from
81	  the rendering alone.
82	
83	* The remaining **~7%** are terms nested inside a *shared* abbreviated term
84	  (e.g. `pk(~ska.3)` inside one `sign(...)` threaded through an edge and drawn as
85	  `SI` in two facts). These are **measurement artifacts** of validating by
86	  *expanding* abbreviations before counting: expansion turns one edge-shared
87	  occurrence into several. The occurrence gate counts occurrences in the actual
88	  constraint system (an edge-shared message counts once), so these are not true
89	  rule violations -- a direct search finds **no** graph where such a term appears
90	  literally >=2x and is left un-abbreviated.
91	
92	Net: outside AC/DH-normalised sub-terms the rule is exact (confirmed by
93	controlled probe and by the AC-free portion of the corpus); the one honest gap
94	is the AC/DH occurrence-over-normal-form count.
95	
96	## What was implemented (`graph-clean`)
97	
98	`abbrev::select(roots) -> Vec<Term>` implements the rule: enumerate every
99	non-tuple sub-term of `roots` with `render_len() >= 10`, keep those occurring
100	>= 2 times, return them shortest-first (bottom-up). Thresholds are exposed as
101	`MIN_ABBREV_LEN=10`, `MIN_ABBREV_OCC=2` and via `select_with(roots,min_len,
102	min_occ)` for probing. `roots` must be the full multiset of constraint-system
103	terms (graph node-fact arguments **and** sequent terms) because occurrence is a
104	whole-system count. Feeding the result through the existing `Abbreviator`
105	reproduces the legend end-to-end.
106	
107	`term::Term` gained `is_tuple()` and `render_len()` (Unicode-scalar count of the
108	full rendering, matching the observed 10-char boundary).
109	
110	Tests (`tests/abbrev_tests.rs`, all green) reproduce each controlled probe:
111	`select_length_boundary_is_ten`,
112	`select_requires_two_occurrences_even_for_long_terms`,
113	`select_never_abbreviates_a_tuple`, `select_then_name_reproduces_probe_legend`,
114	`select_counts_nested_occurrences`, `select_with_custom_thresholds`. Full suite
115	is 24 tests incl. the 12 022-payload byte-exact roundtrip.
116	
117	## Minor open detail
118	A numeric-only constant (`'12345678'`, no alphabetic chars) gets an **empty
119	prefix**, so its abbreviation name is a bare number -- observed as `2` for the
120	first such entry. The starting index for empty-prefix names was not pinned down

import re, glob, os

    txt=open(f).read()
    for line in txt.splitlines():
        if 'shape="record"' in line and '\\l' in line:
            print("FILE", f)
            print(line[:600])
            print("...")
            found+=1
            break
    if found>=3: break
PY

content.text: I can see the accumulated-column effect directly: in `001113f65abfdda3.dot` node n6, the info cell `#i1 : Resp_1[Accept( ~ey, KD1 ), Sid(...)]` has its own flat width ~78 (< 87) yet wraps, because it sits deep on the line (preceded by a 3-cell premise group). Let me set up the live-probe pipeline to pin the true rule. First my scratchpad and a serve helper.

input.command:
SP=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
mkdir -p $SP
cat > $SP/serve.sh <<'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Serve a .spthy in the HS interactive UI on a given port (--port= form).
export PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:$PATH"
BIN="/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work/install/x86_64-linux-tinfo6/ec0cb11b1bfcf8776d45e0357bbc6d6ff2077f9222735af22115429c8cdfcef1/9.6.7/bin/tamarin-prover"
case "$1" in
  start)
    d=$(mktemp -d); cp "$2" "$d/";
    nohup "$BIN" interactive "$d" --port="$3" >/tmp/wrapsrv_$3.log 2>&1 &
    echo $! > /tmp/wrapsrv_$3.pid; sleep 4; echo "up on $3 pid $(cat /tmp/wrapsrv_$3.pid)";;
  stop) kill $(cat /tmp/wrapsrv_$2.pid) 2>/dev/null; rm -f /tmp/wrapsrv_$2.pid; echo stopped;;
esac
EOF
chmod +x $SP/serve.sh
echo "wrote serve.sh"
ls -la $SP

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Isolated per-theory injective scraper.
set -u
SP=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
CORP=$SP/corpus
ORACLE=/home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/injective/oracle/hs_oracle.sh
OUT=$SP/corpus_results.tsv
LOG=$SP/driver.log
BUDGET=${BUDGET:-200}          # seconds per theory
: > "$OUT"; : > "$LOG"
port=3500
while IFS=$'\t' read -r uid rel; do
  port=$((port+1))
  dir="$CORP/$uid"
  slog="$SP/srvlog_$uid.txt"
  echo "[$(date +%T)] START $uid ($rel) port $port" >> "$LOG"
  ORACLE_TIMEOUT=$BUDGET "$ORACLE" interactive "$dir" --port=$port --no-logging > "$slog" 2>&1 &
  spid=$!
  # wait for readiness: index page lists a /thy/ link, OR server died
  ready=0
  for i in $(seq 1 $BUDGET); do
    if ! kill -0 "$spid" 2>/dev/null; then break; fi
    idx=$(curl -s -m 3 "http://127.0.0.1:$port/" 2>/dev/null)
    if echo "$idx" | grep -q "/thy/"; then ready=1; break; fi
    sleep 1
  done
  if [ "$ready" = 1 ]; then
    res=$(python3 "$SP/scrape_one.py" "$port" 2>>"$LOG")
    echo -e "$uid\t$res" >> "$OUT"
    echo "[$(date +%T)] DONE  $uid -> $res" >> "$LOG"
  else
    echo -e "$uid\tLOAD_FAIL_OR_TIMEOUT" >> "$OUT"
    echo "[$(date +%T)] FAIL  $uid (not ready in ${BUDGET}s)" >> "$LOG"
  fi
  kill "$spid" 2>/dev/null
  pkill -P "$spid" 2>/dev/null
  # ensure port freed
  for j in $(seq 1 5); do kill -0 "$spid" 2>/dev/null || break; sleep 1; done
  kill -9 "$spid" 2>/dev/null
done < "$CORP/MANIFEST.tsv"
echo "[$(date +%T)] ALL DONE" >> "$LOG"

content.text: `refresh.py` is a dirty-room integration helper (writes into `crates/`) — not mine to run; I'll ignore it. Let me write my own autoprove+fetch driver and validate it end-to-end on the known-good `wrap.spthy`.

input.command:
SP=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
cat > $SP/drive.py <<'PY'
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# Autoprove each lemma of a served theory and fetch its proof-state graph DOT.
# Usage: drive.py <port> <lemma1> [lemma2 ...]   -> prints "LEMMA\t<dotpath>" lines
import sys, re, json, urllib.request, os
PORT=sys.argv[1]; LEMMAS=sys.argv[2:]
BASE=f"http://127.0.0.1:{PORT}"
OUT=os.environ.get("OUTDIR","/tmp/probe_dots"); os.makedirs(OUT,exist_ok=True)
def get(path, timeout=300):
    with urllib.request.urlopen(BASE+path, timeout=timeout) as r:
        return r.read().decode("utf-8","replace")
def latest_index():
    idx=get("/")
    ms=re.findall(r'/thy/trace/(\d+)/', idx)
    return max(int(x) for x in ms) if ms else 1
for L in LEMMAS:
    i=latest_index()
    # autoprove this lemma from the current latest theory
    try:
        r=get(f"/thy/trace/{i}/autoprove/idfs/0/False/proof/{L}")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"{L}\tAUTOPROVE_ERR {e}"); continue
    # response is JSON with a redirect to the new theory index
    ni=i
    m=re.search(r'/thy/trace/(\d+)/', r)
    if m: ni=int(m.group(1))
    try:
        dot=get(f"/thy/trace/{ni}/interactive-graph-def/proof/{L}/_")
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"{L}\tGRAPH_ERR {e}"); continue
    p=os.path.join(OUT,f"{L}.dot")
    open(p,"w").write(dot)
    print(f"{L}\t{p}\t{len(dot)}")
PY
echo "wrote drive.py"

Let me generate a probe theory that varies premise width, preceding-conclusion width, rule-name width, and trailing content independently.

input.command:
SP=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
$SP/serve.sh stop 3210 >/dev/null 2>&1
cat > $SP/gen_probe.py <<'PY'
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# Generate a probe theory. Each rule -> one node whose layout we read.
# Target conclusion T(<'a'(P), 'y'>): flat = len(funct)+P+13.
# We vary: premise width, preceding-conclusion width, rule-name width, trailing.
import sys
rules=[]; lemmas=[]; meta=[]
def A(name): return name  # action fact name == rule name

def add(name, prem, concl, note):
    # prem, concl: lists of fact strings
    p=", ".join(prem) if prem else ""
    c=", ".join(concl)
    rules.append(f"rule {name}:\n  [ {p} ] --[ {name}() ]-> [ {c} ]\n")
    lemmas.append(f'lemma l_{name}: exists-trace "Ex #i. {name}() @ #i"')
    meta.append((name,note))

def const(n):  # public constant of length n (won't abbreviate at occ 1)
    return "'" + "a"*n + "'"
def tup(P):    # target tuple <'a'(P), 'y'>
    return f"Out(<{const(P)},'y'>)"

# --- Exp1: baseline lone conclusion, sweep P around 87 (funct Out=3 -> flat P+16) ---
for P in [69,70,71,72,73]:
    add(f"B{P}", ["Fr(~s)"], [f"Out(<{const(P)},'y'>)"], f"baseline lone-concl P={P} flat={P+16}")

# --- Exp2: lone conclusion at fixed P=71 (flat 87, borderline no-wrap), grow premise pad ---
# premise In('a'(m)) width = m+8 as a cell. Does bigger premise make Out wrap?
for m in [1,10,20,30,40,50,60,80]:
    add(f"PM{m}", ["Fr(~s)", f"In({const(m)})"], [f"Out(<{const(71)},'y'>)"],
        f"premize pad m={m} (Out flat 87 fixed); premise cell In flat={m+8}")

# --- Exp3: target is 2nd conclusion; preceding conclusion Pre('a'(y)) of varied width ---
# target fixed Out flat 87 (P=71). Preceding concl width varies.
for y in [1,10,20,30,40,60]:
    add(f"CC{y}", ["Fr(~s)"], [f"C({const(y)})", f"Out(<{const(71)},'y'>)"],
        f"preceding-concl y={y} (C cell flat={y+5}); Out flat87 2nd concl")

# --- Exp4: rule-name width (info cell) varies; lone concl Out flat 87 ---
for L in [1,10,20,40]:
    nm="R"+"x"*(L-1) if L>1 else "R"
    add(nm, ["Fr(~s)"], [f"Out(<{const(71)},'y'>)"], f"rulename len={L}; Out flat87 lone concl")

# --- Exp5: lookahead — target Out flat 87 then a trailing conclusion of width z ---
for z in [1,20,60]:
    add(f"LA{z}", ["Fr(~s)"], [f"Out(<{const(71)},'y'>)", f"Tr({const(z)})"],
        f"trailing z={z}; does trailing change Out(flat87) decision?")

open(sys.argv[1],"w").write(
"theory Probe\nbegin\n\n"+ "\n".join(rules) + "\n" + "\n".join(lemmas) + "\n\nend\n")
open(sys.argv[1]+".meta","w").write("\n".join(f"{n}\t{d}" for n,d in meta))
print("rules:", len(rules))
print("\n".join(l.split(':')[0].replace('lemma ','') for l in lemmas))
PY

content.text: Important correction: `In('const')` would spawn a `!KU` node giving the constant a 2nd occurrence, triggering abbreviation and corrupting my width control. Conclusion cells are clean (produced once, no consumer). Let me use distinct short atoms for premise pads and keep target/preceding/trailing as conclusions.

input.command:
SP=/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad
cat > $SP/gen_probe.py <<'PY'
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
rules=[]; lemmas=[]; meta=[]
def add(name, prem, concl, note):
    p=", ".join(prem) if prem else ""
    c=", ".join(concl)
    rules.append(f"rule {name}:\n  [ {p} ] --[ {name}() ]-> [ {c} ]\n")
    lemmas.append(f'lemma l_{name}: exists-trace "Ex #i. {name}() @ #i"')
    meta.append((name,note))
def const(n): return "'" + "a"*n + "'"
def atoms(m):  # m distinct 2-char public constants, tuple
    al="abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz"
    els=[f"'{al[i//26]}{al[i%26]}'" for i in range(m)]
    return "<" + ",".join(els) + ">"
TGT=lambda: f"Out(<{const(71)},'y'>)"   # flat 87, borderline NOT wrapping when alone

# Exp1: baseline lone conclusion, sweep P (Out flat=P+16)
for P in [69,70,71,72,73]:
    add(f"B{P}", ["Fr(~s)"], [f"Out(<{const(P)},'y'>)"], f"lone-concl P={P} flat={P+16}")
# Exp2: lone concl fixed flat87, premise pad = tuple of m distinct atoms
for m in [0,2,5,10,15,20,30]:
    prem=["Fr(~s)"] + ([f"In({atoms(m)})"] if m>0 else [])
    add(f"PM{m}", prem, [TGT()], f"premise atoms m={m}; Out flat87 lone concl")
# Exp3: target=2nd concl, preceding concl C('a'(y)) clean (occurs once)
for y in [0,5,15,25,35,50,70]:
    concl=([f"C({const(y)})"] if y>0 else []) + [TGT()]
    add(f"CC{y}", ["Fr(~s)"], concl, f"preceding-concl y={y}; C cell flat={y+5 if y else 0}; Out flat87")
# Exp4: rule-name width; lone concl flat87
for L in [1,10,20,40]:
    nm="R"+"x"*(L-1) if L>1 else "R"
    add(nm, ["Fr(~s)"], [TGT()], f"rulename len={L}; Out flat87 lone concl")
# Exp5: lookahead — target flat87 1st concl, trailing Tr('a'(z))
for z in [1,25,60]:
    add(f"LA{z}", ["Fr(~s)"], [TGT(), f"Tr({const(z)})"], f"trailing z={z}; test lookahead on Out flat87")

open(sys.argv[1],"w").write("theory Probe\nbegin\n\n"+ "\n".join(rules) + "\n" + "\n".join(lemmas) + "\n\nend\n")
open(sys.argv[1]+".meta","w").write("\n".join(f"{n}\t{d}" for n,d in meta))
print("rules:", len(rules), "->", " ".join(n for n,_ in meta))

python3 $SP/gen_probe.py $SP/probe1.spthy
# Analyzer: for a lemma's dot, find the record node with info cell containing rule name,
# extract each cell text and whether it wraps (contains \l).
cat > $SP/an.py <<'PY'
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys, re
def cells_of(label):
    # strip outer { } ; split groups by top-level | at depth (braces)
    # We just want each "<nK> TEXT" cell. Find them by regex on port markers.
    # A cell text runs from after "<nK> " to the next "|<nK+1> " or group close "}".
    return label
def visual_len(s):
    # un-escape record metachars for column counting; drop \l ; count   as 1
    s=s.replace("\\l","")
    s=s.replace(" "," ")
    for a,b in [("\\<","<"),("\\>",">"),("\\{","{"),("\\}","}"),("\\|","|")]:
        s=s.replace(a,b)
    return len(s)
for path in sys.argv[1:]:
    name=path.split("/")[-1][2:-4]  # l_NAME.dot -> NAME
    txt=open(path).read()
    recs=re.findall(r'n\d+\[shape="record",label="(.*?)",fillcolor=', txt)
    tgt=None
    for lab in recs:
        if f": {name}[" in lab or f": {name}]" in lab or f": {name} " in lab:
            tgt=lab; break
    if tgt is None:
        # fallback: the record whose info contains the rule name token
        for lab in recs:
            if name in lab: tgt=lab; break
    if tgt is None:
        print(name,"NO_RECORD"); continue
    # split into cells: sequence of <nK> ... 
    parts=re.split(r'<n\d+> ', tgt)
    # parts[0] is leading braces; cells are parts[1:], each may end with } or }} or |
    cellinfo=[]
    for pc in parts[1:]:
        # cut trailing group/label closers and separators
        c=re.sub(r'[}|]*$','',pc)
        wrapped = "\\l" in pc
        cellinfo.append((wrapped, visual_len(c), c[:40]))
    # report the Out target cell (contains "Out(" or "Out( ") specifically the tuple one
    out_cells=[ci for ci in cellinfo if ci[2].startswith("Out(") ]
    tag=" ".join(f"{'W' if w else 'f'}:{L}" for w,L,_ in cellinfo)
    print(f"{name}\tcells[{tag}]")
PY

import sys

    rules.append(f"rule {name}:\n  [ Fr(~s) ] --[ {name}() ]-> [ {', '.join(concl)} ]\n")
    lemmas.append(f'lemma l_{name}: exists-trace "Ex #i. {name}() @ #i"')
    meta.append((name,note))

import sys, re, glob, os
def analyze(path):
    name=os.path.basename(path)[2:-4]
    txt=open(path).read()
    recs=re.findall(r'n\d+\[shape="record",label="(.*?)",fillcolor=', txt)
    tgt=None
    for lab in recs:
        if f": {name}[" in lab: tgt=lab; break
    if tgt is None: return name,"NORECORD"
    parts=re.split(r'(<n\d+> )', tgt)
    # reconstruct cells: after each '<nK> ' marker up to next marker
    cells=[]
    toks=re.split(r'<n\d+> ', tgt)
    for pc in toks[1:]:
        c=re.sub(r'[}|]*$','',pc)
        cells.append(("W" if "\\l" in pc else "f", pc))
    # only conclusion cells: those whose text starts with Fa(/Fb(/Fc(
    concl=[(wf) for wf,pc in cells]
    tags=[]
    for wf,pc in cells:
        head=re.sub(r'\\l.*','',pc)[:6]
        tags.append(f"{wf}:{head.strip()}")
    return name, " ".join(tags)
rows=[analyze(p) for p in sys.argv[1:]]
for n,t in sorted(rows): print(f"{n}\t{t}")
PY

The rule is now sharp for premise/conclusion cells. Let me verify it by hand against the Wide record, then probe the info cell (which appears to wrap at flat 76/52 < 87, so it has a different budget).

The Wide record check confirms it exactly:
- `budget = max(87 − Σ(other cells in same group), 20)`, wrap iff flat > budget:
  - Ack: max(87−(68+14),20)=20; flat 25 > 20 → **wraps** ✓
  - Big: max(87−(25+14),20)=48; flat 68 > 48 → **wraps** ✓
  - Out(h): max(87−(25+68),20)=20; flat 14 → **fits** ✓
  - In (premise): max(87−10,20)=77; flat 67 → **fits** ✓

Now let me pin the info-cell rule via corpus + live probes.

def visual_len(s):
    s=s.replace("\\l","").replace(" ","x")
    for a,b in [("\\<","<"),("\\>",">"),("\\{","{"),("\\}","}"),("\\|","|")]:
        s=s.replace(a,b)
    return len(s)
def info_of(path):
    name=os.path.basename(path)[2:-4]
    txt=open(path).read()
    recs=re.findall(r'n\d+\[shape="record",label="(.*?)",fillcolor=', txt)
    tgt=[l for l in recs if f": {name}[" in l]
    if not tgt: return None
    lab=tgt[0]
    cells=re.split(r'<n\d+> ',lab)[1:]
    # info cell: the one containing ": NAME["
    info=None; prem=[]; concl=[]
    for c in cells:
        cc=re.sub(r'[}|]*$','',c)
        if f": {name}[" in c: info=c
    # compute premise/concl flats: cells before info are prem, after are concl
    idx=[i for i,c in enumerate(cells) if f": {name}[" in c][0]
    prem=cells[:idx]; concl=cells[idx+1:]
    def flat(c):
        cc=re.sub(r'[}|]*$','',c)
        # unwrap
        cc=cc.replace("\\l","")
        cc=re.sub(r'( )+',' ',cc)  # approx; but for non-wrapped it's exact
        for a,b in [("\\<","<"),("\\>",">"),("\\{","{"),("\\}","}"),("\\|","|")]:
            cc=cc.replace(a,b)
        return cc
    def uwlen(c):
        cc=flat(c)
        return len(cc)
    infoflat=None
    ic=re.sub(r'[}|]*$','',info)
    # unwrap info: remove \l and &nbsp runs (each &nbsp=1 col but indent added; to get FLAT, remove the indent entirely and the \l)
    icf=ic.replace("\\l","")
    icf=re.sub(r'( )+','',icf)  # remove indent to reconstruct flat
    for a,b in [("\\<","<"),("\\>",">"),("\\{","{"),("\\}","}"),("\\|","|")]:
        icf=icf.replace(a,b)
    infoflat=len(icf)
    sprem=sum(uwlen(c) for c in prem)
    sconcl=sum(uwlen(c) for c in concl)
    wrapped = "\\l" in info
    return (name, wrapped, infoflat, sprem, sconcl)
rows=[]
for p in glob.glob(SP+"/p5_dots/*.dot"):
    r=info_of(p)
    if r: rows.append(r)
import collections
groups=collections.defaultdict(list)
for name,wr,iflat,sp,sc in rows:
    g=re.match(r'I([ABC])',name).group(1)
    w=int(re.match(r'I[ABC](\d+)',name).group(1))
    groups[g].append((w,wr,iflat,sp,sc))
for g in "ABC":

    sp=gg[0][3]; sc=gg[0][4]
    print(f"config {g}: Sprem={sp} Sconcl={sc}")
    # find boundary

    print(f"   -> info boundary (last fit flat, first wrap flat) = {bnd}")
    print(f"   -> 87-Sprem={87-sp}, 87-Sconcl={87-sc}, 87-(Sprem+Sconcl)={87-sp-sc}")
